summary: Soundboard recording with a pleasant, slightly gritty mix captures an outstanding May‑27‑1993 show highlighted by a crushing Shakedown, masterful Masterpiece and the fiery Picasso→Fire combo.
review: The performance is widely praised as one of the best of ’93 – tight first‑set work, a monster Shakedown, soaring Masterpiece, and a memorable Picasso > Fire sequence capped by a “better than TV” Gloria. The SBD source is generally clean and lively, though several listeners note a low‑end muddiness and a touch of grittiness that gives the tape character but can mask some instrument detail.

ai_rating.rationale: Most reviewers rate the show 4‑5 stars and call it a top‑1993 performance; recording quality is good with minor mix quirks, leading to an overall strong combined rating.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Generally clear and lively soundboard, but several notes of a slight grittiness, low instrument levels vs. high master level, and minimal audience bleed that can make the mix feel a bit flat at times.

show_quality.setlist_flow: First set is tight and energetic, delivering several of the year's best renditions; the second set offers surprising twists (Picasso→Fire, UJB sandwich) and a soaring Gloria finale, though a few moments (e.g., Wharf Rat) feel uneven.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Praised for powerful guitar tone, banjo‑like phrasing on Cumberland, and a scorching solo that many call one of his best.
band_member_comments.Phil: Mixed – some reviewers love his vocal on “High Time,” while others dismiss his solo‑band material as weak; overall bass work isn’t specifically critiqued.
band_member_comments.Bob: Vocals noted as thin on Gloria by one reviewer, but the “better than TV” shout is celebrated as a highlight.
band_member_comments.Keys: Vince’s piano receives strong praise for excellent playing and helping shape the new ’93 sound; Brent is later lauded by a reviewer who calls him irreplaceable.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill’s drumming is generally solid; post‑drum “Other One” transition is called flat by one critic.
